thread.section_title: News in Sarasota, FL | Sarasota Herald-Tribune
thread.title: Moody, Rep. Vern Buchanan blast FEMA over discrimination against Trump supporters
thread.title_full: Moody, Rep. Vern Buchanan blast FEMA over discrimination against Trump supporters

author: , Sarasota Herald-Tribune
title: Moody, Rep. Vern Buchanan blast FEMA over discrimination against Trump supporters
text: Enraged by findings that Federal Emergency Management Agency could have discriminated against Republican Florida hurricane victims, Florida Attorney General Ashley Moody and U.S. Rep. Vern Buchanan chastised the agency during a news conference on Anna Maria Island on Friday. Moody filed a lawsuit against the agency on Nov. 13 following reports that a FEMA supervisor directed aid workers in Lake Placid to avoid homes that displayed Donald Trump presidential campaign signs. At least 20 Florida homes are believed to have been affected. Republican lawmakers interrogated FEMA Administrator Deanne Criswell during a committee hearing that ran for nearly 12 hours on Tuesday. Criswell indicated the situation was an insolated incident. But Moody alleged a whistleblower has given rise to suspicion of a broader culture of discrimination within the agency. Criswell issued a statement on Nov. 9 indicating a FEMA supervisor was fired over the situation, saying the actions were "a clear violation of FEMA’s core values and principles to help people regardless of their political affiliation. This was reprehensible. "I want to be clear to all of my employees and the American people, this type of behavior and action will not be tolerated at FEMA and we will hold people accountable if they violate these standards of conduct." At the press conference, Moody said, "We don't wait for the federal agencies to start digging into their investigations, we do it ourselves, because you've got to hold them accountable. That's what we are doing here in Florida, and that's why I was proud to bring this lawsuit so quickly." She added: "First and foremost it wasn't whether it happened, it was almost admitted right off the bat... I think the question is now how did this happen? And what is being done about it?" Moody said her office is still identifying potential additional defendants in the lawsuit who could have also been discriminated against by FEMA during the hurricane recovery process. Buchanan supports Moody's lawsuit against FEMA discrimination The press conference was held at a sand disposal site in the city of Bradenton Beach. Much of the city was flooded and buried in sand during Hurricane Helene , and sustained additional wind and storm surge damage during Hurricane Milton weeks later . Buchanan, who was re-elected to a 10th term in congress during the general election , said he believes discrimination concerns at FEMA will end under new Republican leadership after Trump is sworn in. He was one of 54 U.S. House of Representatives members who recently signed a letter to Criswell demanding answers and accountability. "Anybody that is of a particular mindset that's not working for everybody they're gone," Buchanan said. "We are going to get rid of them. It's a different environment in Washington now, we are not going to put up with nonsense." Buchanan supports Bondi, talks government spending The congressman also expressed desire for a shakeup throughout more federal government agencies during Friday's news conference, and cast his support behind former Florida attorney general Pam Bondi as Trump's choice for U.S. attorney general after Rep. Matt Gaetz withdrew from consideration under pressure. "I feel very confident she's going to be our next attorney general," Buchanan said. "It's nice to see a lot of folks from Florida getting these opportunities. I think it's pretty exciting, we've got a lot of talent here." Buchanan is the most tenured member of the U.S. House of Ways and Means Committee, and serves as chair on its subcommittee for health. He said his eyes are set on addressing losses from fraud in the Medicare program, and concerns about federal spending. "We're not going to screw with Medicare or social security; there might be some adjustments," Buchanan said. "But we can't continue to go down the road and spend $2 trillion more than we take in." "A lot of youngsters, we're spending their money. It's their money, they are counting on their money," he said. "The other side of it is our interest on the debt is a trillion dollars. That's wrong. That's immoral. We've got to get this under control."
